prompt: A depiction of the machine learning concept 'gradient descent' rendered as a Japanese ukiyo-e woodblock print from the Edo period (1603–1868). The scene shows a lone traveler descending a steep, layered mountain path toward a calm valley, symbolizing iterative optimization toward a minimum. The composition uses a high, flattened perspective with asymmetrical balance, inspired by Hokusai and Hiroshige. Stylized contour lines define the terrain, with repeated wave-like ridges echoing loss surface contours. The color palette is limited to natural mineral tones — indigo blues, muted greens, and soft earth browns — with visible flat color regions and subtle bokashi-style gradients in the sky and slopes. The traveler takes small, deliberate steps downward, reinforcing the idea of incremental updates. Negative space (ma) is used to emphasize direction and movement. No text, no modern elements, no diagrams — purely a historically authentic ukiyo-e scene where the concept of gradient descent is visually encoded through terrain, motion, and composition.
key_visual_characteristics: Bold contour lines and flat color planes with limited mineral palette
Use of negative space (ma) and bokashi gradient shading
